I have published in academic peer-reviewed channels.
I am recognised internationally as an authority on Indonesian-context Cambridge teaching in my subject.
I mentor peer specialists into international publication and presentation.
I shape Cambridge international community discourse in my subject.

activity.task: Deliver a keynote at an international Cambridge event OR publish in an academic peer-reviewed channel.

xen-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to maintain a structured reading practice in subject-discipline research — turning key papers into network briefings or pacing changes. A specialist at this stage subscribes to peer-reviewed journals, reads ~2 hours per week, and brings 1-2 research insights to network briefings per quarter. For example, an English Language specialist subscribes to the journal English in Education + Cambridge English Online; she reads weekly, identifies recent research on second-language writing development, and brings the insight to a network Subject Leader briefing. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + Eduversal Domain 6 (research-engaged practice). At this stage, the specialist reads and briefs but does not yet drive scheme-of-work decisions through research.
keyTakeaways: Subscribe to subject-discipline peer-reviewed journals.
Read ~2 hours per week.
Bring 1-2 research insights to network briefings per quarter.
selfAssessment: I subscribe to at least one peer-reviewed subject-discipline journal.
I maintain a ~2-hour-per-week reading practice.
I bring 1-2 research insights to network briefings per quarter.
I keep a personal log of research insights with potential network application.
I have shared at least one research insight with HQ Director this term.

activity.task: Subscribe to one peer-reviewed subject-discipline journal. Maintain a ~2-hour-per-week reading practice for one term. Bring 2 research insights to network briefings.
activity.output: Subscription record + reading log + briefing notes.

xen-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently use research to drive scheme-of-work decisions. A specialist at this stage maintains a structured reading practice, translates research into specific intervention design, and tracks year-on-year network capability improvement based on research-driven decisions. For example, a Mathematics specialist reads research on visualisation in algebra learning, designs a network-wide algebra-visualisation CPD, implements across 8 partner schools, and tracks year-on-year improvement in algebraic manipulation performance. After one year, network Year 9 algebraic manipulation performance improves by 11 percentile points. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5. At this stage, the specialist drives scheme-of-work decisions through research effectively but does not yet shape network-wide research engagement standards.
keyTakeaways: Translate research into specific intervention design.
Track year-on-year network capability improvement based on research-driven decisions.
Make research-driven decisions visible — outcomes prove the practice.
selfAssessment: I drive scheme-of-work decisions through research insights.
I track year-on-year network capability improvement based on research-driven decisions.
I can describe at least 3 research-driven interventions I designed this year.
Network capability has improved measurably during my tenure.
I share research-driven design methodology with peer specialists.

activity.task: Choose one research insight from your reading. Design a network-wide intervention based on it. Implement across at least 5 partner schools. Track outcome.
activity.output: Intervention design + implementation log + outcome analysis.

xen-4_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists shape network-wide research engagement methodology. A specialist at this stage develops research engagement frameworks that other specialists adopt, contributes to Eduversal research-engagement standards across multiple subjects, and trains specialists in research-driven design methodology. For example, an English Language specialist with 6 years' Eduversal experience develops a structured research engagement framework: subscription protocol (peer-reviewed journals + professional associations), reading rhythm (~2 hours weekly + monthly insights logs), translation protocol (research insight → potential network application → intervention design → outcome tracking). The framework is adopted by 5 peer specialists across other subjects. After one year of network-wide adoption, research-driven scheme-of-work amendments increase from 4 per year to 17 per year network-wide, and at least three Cambridge result improvements are directly traceable to research-driven CPD pathway revisions. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 + Eduversal Network Research Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network research-engagement methodology but does not yet contribute peer-reviewed academic publications.
keyTakeaways: Develop reusable research engagement frameworks.
Contribute to Eduversal research-engagement standards.
Train specialists in research-driven design methodology.
selfAssessment: I have developed research engagement frameworks other specialists adopt.
I contribute to Eduversal research-engagement standards.
I train other specialists in research-driven design methodology.
I have presented research engagement methodology to HQ Directors.
I am known within the network for research engagement expertise.

activity.task: Develop a research engagement framework. Submit to peer specialists. Train at least one peer specialist in the methodology.

xen-4_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists position the Eduversal network as a research-contributor (not just research-consumer) in their subject discipline at the international level. A specialist at this stage publishes peer-reviewed academic research, presents at international academic conferences in her subject discipline, and is recognised within the international subject-discipline community as an authority on Indonesian-context Cambridge teaching. For example, a Chemistry specialist with 10 years' Eduversal experience publishes a peer-reviewed article on 'language demand in Cambridge IGCSE Chemistry assessment for second-language candidates in South-East Asia' in a respected Chemistry education journal, presents at the regional International Conference on Chemical Education (ICCE) on Indonesian-context Cambridge Chemistry teaching, and contributes a chapter to a forthcoming book on Cambridge teaching practice in South-East Asia. The Indonesian-context research insights enrich the international subject-discipline community's understanding of Cambridge teaching in second-language contexts. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 + 5.4.
keyTakeaways: Position the Eduversal network as a research contributor in your subject discipline.
Publish peer-reviewed academic research.
Present at international academic conferences.
selfAssessment: I have published peer-reviewed academic research in my subject discipline.
I have presented at an international academic conference in my subject discipline.
I am recognised within the international subject-discipline community.
I mentor peer specialists into academic research engagement.
I shape the subject-discipline research conversation about Indonesian-context Cambridge teaching.

activity.task: Submit a peer-reviewed academic research paper or international conference proposal. If accepted, develop and deliver.

provenance.framework_sources: Cambridge Teacher Standards 2023, Eduversal Specialist Competency Framework, Danielson Framework for Teaching
provenance.format_baseline: Same protocol as Part 1 + 161 + 35 hand-authored TH/AH entries
provenance.audit_trigger: INDEX.md health snapshot 2026-05-08 — specialists track at 0% Indonesia-context density vs hand-authored TH/AH; this Part 2 closes the remaining 64 entries
provenance.completion_state: Part 1 (32 entries: csm + cof + tpd × 4) + Part 2 (48 entries: cqa + nls + xen × 4) = 80 entries. The csm-1_practitioner and beyond is 16 entries × 4 levels missing in Part 1's structure — recount below.
